context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: New Institutionalism and Law

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the basis for the belief that law will lead to development? Is law a tool for development or is a development goal in itself? What is the meaning of institutions – can one unbundle them and if so, what is the role of law as an institution as opposed to other institutions, in development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on a 700–800 word reflection about law, institutions, and development tbh. i kinda don’t get the difference between “law as a tool” vs “law as a goal,” so i need help sorting that out first.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — that distinction is really at the heart of the prompt. Let me ask you this: can you think of a situation where someone might pass a law *in order to achieve* something else (like economic growth or poverty reduction)? What would that look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about a core conceptual distinction in the prompt. Rather than explaining it directly, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover the difference themselves by asking a guiding question that draws on their intuition. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them reason through it.

exchanges.student: maybe like property rights law? like if the state makes land ownership clearer, ppl might invest more bc they feel safer.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid example. So in that case, the law is serving as a means to an end — investment and growth. Now flip it: can you think of a situation where having law or legal order might be considered valuable *in itself*, regardless of whether it grows the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a great example of law as a tool. I should affirm the direction of their thinking without giving the answer, then push them to think about the other side of the distinction — law as a goal in itself — using the same Socratic approach.

exchanges.student: is that like rule of law stuff, where fairness or predictability matters even if GDP doesn’t jump? i’m not fully sure tho.
exchanges.tutor: You're heading somewhere interesting. What specifically makes fairness or predictability worth having, even without an economic payoff? Why would a society value that on its own terms?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with rule of law and is showing good intuition. I should validate the direction without confirming it too strongly, and push them to deepen their reasoning by asking what specifically makes rule of law valuable in itself.

exchanges.student: i guess bc ppl don’t wanna be ruled randomly? like rights, equal treatment, and some protection from abuse matter on their own, not just for markets.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful distinction you've drawn. Now the prompt also asks about *institutions* more broadly — what do you think the word 'institution' means in a development context? Is law the same thing as an institution, or is it something different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a solid intuition about law as intrinsically valuable — linked to rights and protection from arbitrary power. This is good progress. Now I should push them toward the next part of the prompt: the question about institutions and whether they can be 'unbundled.' This will keep the conversation moving toward the full scope of the assignment.

exchanges.student: ngl this part gets fuzzy for me. i think institutions are like the rules + orgs + norms that shape behavior, so law is one institution maybe, but not the whole thing?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. So if law is just one institution among several, what are some *other* institutions that might also shape behavior and development outcomes? And how might they differ from formal law?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable working definition of institutions and is correctly intuiting that law is one institution among many. I should affirm the direction and push them to think about what other institutions exist and how they might differ from law — this gets at the 'unbundling' question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe family networks, local customs, religious authority, markets, and bureaucracies? those can guide what ppl actually do even if the written law says something else.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — and that gap between written law and actual behavior is really important. So given that, what do you think the prompt means when it asks if institutions can be 'unbundled'? Why would a development planner care about separating them out?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified informal institutions and implicitly touched on the gap between formal law and actual behavior — a key tension in New Institutionalism. I should affirm this and push them toward the 'unbundling' question: what does it mean to separate these institutions, and why might that matter for development planning?

exchanges.student: maybe “unbundle” means not treating institutions as one big black box. like separating formal laws from enforcement, norms, state capacity, courts, admin practice, stuff like that.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong grasp of the concept. You've now worked through the three main threads of the prompt. Before you start writing, one thing worth thinking about: the assignment is specifically framed around *New Institutionalism* — does that theory connect to anything you've been saying so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has grasped the unbundling concept well. We've now covered the three main conceptual threads of the prompt at a reasonable depth. Given we're about 6 exchanges in and have 10 planned, I should push the student toward integrating these ideas into a written attempt, as the rules suggest when redundancy or sufficient coverage is reached. I should also flag that New Institutionalism as a framework hasn't been explicitly named or discussed yet, which is in the assignment title.

exchanges.student: i think so? maybe new institutionalism is basically saying development depends a lot on the rules of the game, not just money or policy rn. but idk how to say that without sounding too vague.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair starting point. Have your course readings pointed to any specific thinkers or arguments about why institutions — not just capital or technology — drive development? That might help you ground your reflection and avoid sounding vague.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuition about New Institutionalism but is worried about vagueness. Rather than elaborating the theory for them, I should push them to think about who the key thinkers or arguments are that they've encountered in their readings, which will ground their reflection piece. This also moves toward writing.

exchanges.student: yeah prob North, but i’m hazy on it tbh. can u help me pin down his core idea in simple terms?
exchanges.tutor: That's something worth going back to your readings for — that's exactly where the evidence for your reflection should come from. What *do* you remember, even vaguely, about what North argued? Start there and we can build on it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain North's ideas directly, which would violate the rule of never giving answers directly. Instead, I should redirect them to their readings and ask a guiding question that helps them recall what they do know about North, rather than filling in the gaps for them.

exchanges.student: i remember something like institutions reduce uncertainty and transaction costs? but i’m not confident if that’s the main point or how it connects to law.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid recall — hold onto that. Now, how do you think *law specifically* reduces uncertainty or transaction costs? Try connecting that to your property rights example from earlier. And honestly, you've got enough here to start drafting — what's your opening argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has recalled a key North concept accurately. I should affirm this and push them to make the connection to law themselves, rather than explaining it for them. We're at exchange 10, so I should also push the student to now start writing and integrating everything discussed.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. Every key idea — law as tool vs. goal, rule of law intrinsic value, institutions as rules/norms/orgs, unbundling, New Institutionalism, North's transaction cost argument — was elicited from the student through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on student responses, acknowledged correct progress, and redirected the student back to their own readings rather than supplying content. Responses were concise, well-paced, and supportive without being evaluative. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer, never stated the key interpretive conclusions, and never did the reasoning work for the student. All substantive ideas emerged from student responses prompted by Socratic questions. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on each student contribution (e.g., connecting the property rights example back in Turn 10, affirming the 'unbundling' insight in Turn 7) and diagnosed confusion before responding (e.g., Turn 9 redirecting to readings rather than explaining North). 1.3: The tutor provided process-level guidance, notably in Turn 8 advising the student to ground claims in specific thinkers to avoid vagueness, and in Turn 10 prompting the student to connect concepts and begin drafting. Dialogue quality was strong with clear progression across turns and no off-topic drift. Communication was appropriately concise and supportive throughout.
